02 Types & Coercion — The Source Identification Problem

← Back to lessons

JavaScript has types. It just doesn't enforce them. Understanding the type system and its coercion rules is the difference between "I don't know why this works" and "I know exactly what the engine will do."

The Mental Model: Sources and Fact-Checking

In journalism, a source might say they're "a senior official." But are they? You need to verify. JavaScript's type system works the same way — values claim to be certain types, but the engine sometimes reinterprets them without asking. That reinterpretation is coercion.

TypeScript is the fact-checker that says "You said this was a number, but it's actually a string" before the story goes to print. JavaScript alone publishes first and corrects later.

The 7 Primitive Types

JavaScript has exactly 7 primitive types. Everything else is an object.

typeof "hello"      // "string"
typeof 42           // "number"
typeof 42n          // "bigint"
typeof true         // "boolean"
typeof undefined    // "undefined"
typeof Symbol("id") // "symbol"
typeof null         // "object"  ← THE BUG

typeof null === "object" is a 30-year-old bug. In the original JavaScript implementation, values were stored as a type tag + value. Objects had a type tag of 0, and null was the NULL pointer (0x00). So null's type tag was 0, which typeof read as "object." It can never be fixed because too much code depends on it.

Primitives vs Objects

Primitives are immutable and compared by value. Objects are mutable and compared by reference.

// Primitives: compared by value
"hello" === "hello"   // true
42 === 42             // true

// Objects: compared by reference
[1, 2] === [1, 2]    // false (different objects in memory)
{} === {}             // false

const a = { x: 1 };
const b = a;          // b points to the SAME object
a === b               // true (same reference)

React connection: This is exactly why React uses Object.is() for state comparisons and why you can't mutate state directly. setState({ ...old, newProp }) creates a new reference, which React can detect. Mutating the old object keeps the same reference — React sees no change.

typeof Gotchas

typeof []             // "object"  (arrays are objects)
typeof {}             // "object"
typeof function(){}   // "function" (special case)
typeof null           // "object"  (the bug)
typeof NaN            // "number"  (Not-a-Number is a number?!)
typeof undefined      // "undefined"
typeof undeclaredVar  // "undefined" (doesn't throw!)

Better type checks:

// Check for arrays
Array.isArray([1, 2])         // true

// Check for null
value === null                // direct comparison

// Check for NaN
Number.isNaN(NaN)             // true
Number.isNaN("hello")         // false (unlike global isNaN)

// Check for plain objects
Object.prototype.toString.call(value) === "[object Object]"

Coercion: The Rules

Coercion is not random. It follows a precise algorithm. There are two kinds:

Explicit Coercion (you asked for it)

String(42)          // "42"
Number("42")        // 42
Boolean("")         // false
Boolean("hello")    // true

Implicit Coercion (JavaScript decided for you)

// The + operator with strings triggers string coercion
"5" + 3             // "53" (number → string, concatenation)
"5" - 3             // 2   (string → number, subtraction)

// Why the difference? + is overloaded for strings and numbers.
// - only works with numbers, so JS converts the string.

The rule is simple: + prefers strings. If either operand is a string, the other becomes a string. All other math operators (-, *, /) prefer numbers, so they convert strings to numbers.

The Abstract Equality Algorithm (==)

This is what == actually does, step by step:

// Same type? Use strict equality.
42 == 42            // true (both numbers, no coercion)

// null == undefined is specially defined as true
null == undefined   // true
null == 0           // false (null only equals undefined)

// Number vs String? Convert string to number.
42 == "42"          // true (Number("42") → 42)

// Boolean vs anything? Convert boolean to number FIRST.
true == "1"         // true (true → 1, "1" → 1)
true == "true"      // false (true → 1, Number("true") → NaN)

// Object vs primitive? Call .valueOf() then .toString()
[1] == 1            // true ([1].toString() → "1" → 1)

Always use === unless you have a specific reason to use ==. The only legitimate use case for == is value == null, which catches both null and undefined in one check.

The Falsy Values (Memorize These)

Exactly 8 values are falsy in JavaScript. Everything else is truthy.

Boolean(false)      // false
Boolean(0)          // false
Boolean(-0)         // false
Boolean(0n)         // false (BigInt zero)
Boolean("")         // false (empty string)
Boolean(null)       // false
Boolean(undefined)  // false
Boolean(NaN)        // false

// Everything else is truthy, including:
Boolean([])         // true  (empty array!)
Boolean({})         // true  (empty object!)
Boolean("0")        // true  (non-empty string!)
Boolean("false")    // true  (non-empty string!)

React connection: This is why {arr.length && <Component />} can render 0 on the screen. When arr is empty, arr.length is 0 — a falsy number, but still a valid React child. Use {arr.length > 0 && <Component />} instead.

Number Edge Cases

0.1 + 0.2                 // 0.30000000000000004
0.1 + 0.2 === 0.3         // false
Number.EPSILON             // 2.220446049250313e-16

// Correct float comparison:
Math.abs(0.1 + 0.2 - 0.3) < Number.EPSILON  // true

// NaN is the only value not equal to itself
NaN === NaN               // false
Number.isNaN(NaN)         // true

// Infinity
1 / 0                     // Infinity
-1 / 0                    // -Infinity
Number.isFinite(Infinity) // false

// -0 (negative zero)
-0 === 0                  // true (=== can't tell them apart)
Object.is(-0, 0)          // false (Object.is can)

The Newer Primitives: Symbol and BigInt

Symbol

Symbols are unique identifiers. They're most commonly used as property keys that won't collide with anything.

const id = Symbol("id");
const anotherId = Symbol("id");
id === anotherId  // false (every Symbol is unique)

const user = {
  name: "Trevor",
  [id]: 12345   // Symbol as a property key
};

Object.keys(user)           // ["name"] — Symbols are hidden
Object.getOwnPropertySymbols(user)  // [Symbol(id)]

Where you've seen this: Symbol.iterator is what makes for...of work. Symbol.toPrimitive controls how objects coerce. These "well-known symbols" are JavaScript's internal hooks.

BigInt

const big = 9007199254740993n;  // beyond Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER
typeof big                       // "bigint"

// Can't mix BigInt and Number
big + 1    // TypeError: Cannot mix BigInt and other types
big + 1n   // 9007199254740994n (both BigInt, OK)

Knowledge Check

1. What does "" + 1 + 0 evaluate to?

"10". The empty string makes + a string concatenation. "" + 1 is "1", then "1" + 0 is "10".

2. Why is typeof null === "object" and can it be fixed?

It's a bug from the original JavaScript implementation where values were stored as type tags. Null's representation (0x00) had the same tag as objects (0). It can't be fixed because changing it would break billions of web pages that rely on this behavior.

3. What's the output? [] == ![]

true. ![] is false (arrays are truthy, negated). Now: [] == false. Boolean converts to number: [] == 0. Array converts to primitive: "" == 0. String to number: 0 == 0. true.

4. Why does {arr.length && <Component />} sometimes render "0"?

When arr.length is 0, the && short-circuits and returns 0 (a falsy number). React renders numbers, so it renders "0" on screen. Fix: {arr.length > 0 && <Component />}.

Exercise: Type Coercion Detective

For each expression, predict the result AND explain the coercion steps. Then verify in the console.

1.  "5" - 3
2.  "5" + 3
3.  true + true
4.  "foo" * 3
5.  null + 1
6.  undefined + 1
7.  "3" > "12"
8.  null == undefined
9.  null === undefined
10. NaN === NaN

Bonus: Write a function strictEquals(a, b) that behaves exactly like === but handles NaN and -0 correctly (like Object.is()).
